pages.raw_text:
Does prosperity or hard times tend most to make a
people temperate in drink?


Does heavy drinking sobrety fit most natural into
general prosperity?


Statscians of the London board of trade attribute the
decrease in England's drink bills during the last five
years to the hard times.


But the United States has still greater decline in
drink bills to attribute to prosperity


For the first time in almost decade the annual report
of the commission of internal shows decline
in the amount of whiskey consumed in this country.


The figures made public show that the consumption
of distilled spirits other than those from fruits in the s
cal year 1905 11614732 gallons. The decline from
that of last year of 704,640 gallons.


So the downward tendency to drink, in prosperous
times is not to be accounted for upon the theory Of the
London board of trade.


Prosperity and heavy drinking do necessarily go
gether The contrary is the in this country today.


There has been decided revival of reform sentiment
in the cities all the land Graft exposures have
cited popular spirit for general cleaning up.


The first effect has been upon the Sunday saloons. In
ST. Louis alone in the last four months of 'd reform
Sunday drunks shown by the arrests have decreased
SO per cent.


Gov Folks brave stand for law and order has aroused
such general approval throughout the country that public
officials everywhere have been moved less decided
y to emulate his example, with the result that there is
hardly city in the country in which the lines not
drawn more strictly about the saloon than they
year ago


Temperance and anti saloon agitation before
effectively organized than large share of
the decline is attributable to this.


The figures will encourage temperance advocates to
believe that the for the decline to be found
in the growing law enforcement and the increasing in
telgence and self-care of the people.
